original_abstract: An analytic solution to the multi-target Bayes recursion known as the δ-Generalized Labeled Multi-Bernoulli ( δ-GLMB) filter has been recently proposed by Vo and Vo in [“Labeled Random Finite Sets and Multi-Object Conjugate Priors,” IEEE Trans. Signal Process., vol. 61, no. 13, pp. 3460-3475, 2014]. As a sequel to that paper, the present paper details efficient implementations of the δ-GLMB multi-target tracking filter. Each iteration of this filter involves an update operation and a prediction operation, both of which result in weighted sums of multi-target exponentials with intractably large number of terms. To truncate these sums, the ranked assignment and K-th shortest path algorithms are used in the update and prediction, respectively, to determine the most significant terms without exhaustively computing all of the terms. In addition, using tools derived from the same framework, such as probability hypothesis density filtering, we present inexpensive (relative to the δ-GLMB filter) look-ahead strategies to reduce the number of computations. Characterization of the L1-error in the multi-target density arising from the truncation is presented.
humanized_abstract: An implementation for the analytic solution to the multi-target Bayes recursion known as the δ-Generalized Labeled Multi-Bernoulli ( δ-GLMB) filter proposed by Vo and Vo in [“Labeled Random Finite Sets and Multi-Object Conjugate Priors,” IEEE Trans. Signal Process., vol. 61, no. 13, pp. 3460-3475, 2014]. This paper focuses on the implementation details of the proposed δ-GLMB multi-target tracking filter. This filter iterates between update and prediction stages, and both stages involve computation of a weighted sum of multi-target exponentials that have an unmanageable number of terms. We exploit the structure of the δ-GLMB distribution and utilize techniques derived from ranked assignment and K-th shortest path algorithms in the update stage and prediction stage to obtain a sub-set of the terms with the highest values, in order to obtain a manageable number of terms for computations. We also derive cheap look-ahead schemes from our implementation of the probability hypothesis density (PHD) filter in the same framework. Finally, we give a characterization of the L1-error in multi-target density due to truncation.
